fuzzy_prd:
Our vulnerability-tracking platform ingests raw security-feed documents and needs to derive several clean, query-friendly data structures from them without re-implementing the same parsing logic in every consumer. We need four utility functions: (1) given a raw vulnerability configuration document (the deeply-nested kind we receive from the national feed), extract which vendors and products are affected — deduplicate the pairs and return a mapping from each vendor to its distinct products, sorted; (2) given that vendor-product map, flatten it into a single ordered token list that each subscription filter can match against — the format must use a fixed separator string between vendor and product tokens, look at how the subscription-matching module constructs these tokens and follow the same convention; (3) given the weakness classification entries attached to a vulnerability, return the distinct set of identifier strings sorted in ascending order; and (4) return the fixed alphabet of bucket labels used by the vendor directory page to group vendors — this is a constant sequence that should never change. All four functions are pure transformations and should produce canonical output: sorted keys, sorted arrays, and a consistent separator for the combined tokens.

One small follow-up on the parsing details the team asked about: the standardized component identifier strings live under the key 'cpe23Uri', and they can show up at arbitrary nesting depths inside the configuration document, so the extraction needs to walk the whole thing rather than assume one fixed path. When we split a CPE 2.3 string on colons, the vendor comes from the 4th field (index 3) and the product comes from the 5th (index 4), and those are the exact pieces we should use when building the vendor/product map.

Also, the separator for the flattened subscription tokens is '$PRODUCT$'. The combined form is exactly '<vendor>$PRODUCT$<product>'. Ordering-wise, for each vendor, emit the bare vendor token first, then each combined vendor+product token for that vendor.

And for the vendor directory buckets, the constant sequence is the 26 lowercase letters a–z in alphabetical order, then '@', then digits 0–9 in ascending order. The full string is: 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz@0123456789'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature:
def convert_cpes(conf: dict) -> dict
def flatten_vendors(vendors: dict) -> list
def get_cwes(problems: list) -> list
def get_vendors_letters() -> str

trigger_keywords: how is the vendor product map rendered
JSON output format for CPE extraction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output is canonical JSON: vendor keys are sorted alphabetically, each vendor's product list is sorted alphabetically. An empty document yields '{}\n'. Example: '{"bar": ["baz"], "foo": ["bar", "baz"]}\n'.

trigger_keywords: which field holds the CWE identifier in problem entries
what field to extract from problem objects
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The weakness identifier is in the 'value' field of each problem entry object. Other fields (like 'lang') are ignored. Duplicates are collapsed and results are sorted ascending.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: how the subscription-matching module constructs these tokens
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The flatten_vendors function in the vulnerability utilities, which constructs tokens as '<vendor>' and '<vendor>$PRODUCT$<product>' — the same format used by subscription filters when testing whether a subscriber follows a given vendor or product.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Refer to the web/ directory for the Python vulnerability utility functions and rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.py for the action-to-function mapping.

feature1_convert_cpes.json.description: Extract the vulnerable software identifiers from a vulnerability configuration document. The document embeds one or more standardized component URIs (the CPE 2.3 colon-delimited format) at arbitrary nesting depths under a fixed key. From each URI the vendor and product tokens (the 4th and 5th colon-separated fields) are read, duplicate vendor/product pairs are collapsed, and the result is a mapping from each vendor to the list of its distinct products. An empty document yields an empty mapping.

feature2_flatten_vendors.json.description: Flatten a mapping of vendors to their products into a single ordered token list. For each vendor, first emit the vendor token, then emit one combined token per product formed by joining the vendor and product with a fixed separator string. Vendors are processed in the order they appear in the mapping, and each vendor's products are processed in the given order. An empty mapping yields an empty list.

feature3_get_cwes.json.description: Collect the distinct weakness identifiers from a list of problem entries. Each entry is an object carrying a value field holding the identifier string (alongside other ignored fields such as a language tag). The result is the set of unique identifier values, with duplicates collapsed. An empty input list yields an empty result.

feature4_vendors_letters.json.description: Produce the fixed sequence of index letters used to bucket vendors alphabetically. The sequence is the 26 lowercase Latin letters in order, followed by an at-sign, followed by the ten decimal digits in ascending order. The output is these characters concatenated into a single string. The request carries no parameters.
